Write PLAN.md - project overview and architecture
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
PLAN.md is the living document capturing all design decisions, research summaries, and implementation guidance. It serves as the anchor for the project — the thing that gets updated when decisions change, not the spec. Unlike SPEC.md which is locked, PLAN.md evolves with the project.

**Scope (from the existing file):**
- Section 1: Goals & Scope (problem, solution, non-goals, v1.0 scope table)
- Section 2: Research Summary (4 streams: memory frameworks, prompt compression, provider-native, Rust ecosystem)
- Section 3: Design Principles (cache-stability, library-first, sync core, pluggable traits, idempotent state, format-agnostic)
- Section 4: Public API (ContextPruner facade, builder, types, error type)
- Section 5: Workspace Architecture (crate layout, dependency graph, feature flags)
- Section 6: Algorithm specifications (dedup, purge_errors, stale_file_reads, pipeline)
- Section 7: State & persistence (SessionState, PersistedState, storage path, idempotent rebuild)
- Section 8: Configuration schema (JSONC config, cascade, validation)
- Section 9: Integration patterns (jcode, Claude Code hook, MCP service, rig adapter)
- Section 10: Phased delivery (10 phases with acceptance criteria)
- Section 11: Testing strategy (test pyramid, property tests, snapshot tests, benchmarks)
- Section 12: Risks & mitigation (10 risks identified)
- Section 13: License & clean-room methodology
- Section 14: Locked decisions (9 decisions)
- Section 15: Open questions (Q1-Q7)
- Appendix A: Cargo.toml skeletons
- Appendix B: SPEC.md outline

**Justification:**
PLAN.md documents the WHY behind every decision. When implementing dcp-prune for example, you read SPEC.md to know WHAT to implement, but you read PLAN.md to understand WHY the strategy exists and HOW it fits into the bigger picture.

**Deliverables:**
- Updated PLAN.md with any new decisions
- Must reference SPEC.md as the source of truth for behavior

title: Phase F1: Implement prompt extensions — nudge.rs
description:
# Phase F1: Implement prompt extensions — nudge.rs

## Background
Port of `lib/prompts/extensions/nudge.ts`. Builds dynamic nudge/guidance text appended to compression nudges. Provides the model with runtime context about compressed blocks and message priorities.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-prompts/src/extensions/nudge.rs` (new file, need to create `extensions/` dir)

## Public API
```rust
/// Reads active compressed block IDs from state, formats as "b1, b2, b3..."
/// Produces guidance: "Active compressed blocks: N (b1, b2...). Include placeholders."
pub fn build_compressed_block_guidance(state: &SessionState) -> String;

/// Produces priority guidance listing high/medium priority message refs before anchor
pub fn render_message_priority_guidance(priority_label: &str, refs: &[String]) -> String;

/// Inserts guidance text before the closing </dcp-system-reminder> tag
/// If tag not found, returns nudge_text unchanged
pub fn append_guidance_to_dcp_tag(nudge_text: &str, guidance: &str) -> String;
```

## Implementation
- `build_compressed_block_guidance`: sort active_block_ids, format as "b1, b2, b3", produce multi-line guidance string
- `render_message_priority_guidance`: format list of refs as comma-separated, wrap in template
- `append_guidance_to_dcp_tag`: find `</dcp-system-reminder>` by `rfind()`, splice guidance before it

## Tests
- `test_build_guidance_with_active_blocks` — produces correct format
- `test_build_guidance_with_no_blocks` — returns empty or appropriate message
- `test_render_priority_guidance_high` — high priority refs listed
- `test_append_guidance_to_dcp_tag` — guidance inserted before closing tag
- `test_append_guidance_no_tag` — returns unchanged when tag missing

## Dependencies
- dcp-prompts crate must exist (it does)
- dcp-state for SessionState type
- No other new crate dependencies

## Why this matters
Dynamic guidance in nudges tells the model about existing compressed blocks and message priorities, enabling smarter compression decisions. Without this, the model might try to re-compress already-compressed blocks or miss high-priority messages.

title: Phase C1: Implement dcp-messages query.rs — Message query functions

# Phase C1: Implement dcp-messages query.rs — Message query functions

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/query.ts`. Pure query/inspection functions for message arrays used throughout the codebase. These are foundational — many other modules call them.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/query.rs`

## Public API
```rust
/// Searches backward from start_index (or end) for a non-ignored user message
pub fn get_last_user_message(messages: &[Message], start_index: Option<usize>) -> Option<&Message>;

/// Returns true if message is assistant with at least one completed compress tool part
pub fn message_has_compress(message: &Message) -> bool;

/// A user message is "ignored" if it has no parts, or all parts have ignored: true
pub fn is_ignored_user_message(message: &Message) -> bool;

/// Returns true when mode is "message", protectUserMessages is enabled,
/// and the message is a non-ignored user message
pub fn is_protected_user_message(config: &Config, message: &Message) -> bool;
```

## Implementation notes
- All functions include an `is_valid_message` guard as the first check
- `is_ignored_user_message` is critical: empty user messages and fully-ignored user messages are excluded from nudge/anchor logic
- `message_has_compress` checks for completed compress tool calls — used as signal that compression is happening, which clears all nudge state

## Tests
- `test_get_last_user_message_finds_user` — returns last non-ignored user
- `test_get_last_user_message_skips_ignored` — skips ignored messages
- `test_get_last_user_message_with_start_index` — searches backward from index
- `test_get_last_user_message_none_when_all_ignored` — returns None
- `test_message_has_compress_true` — assistant with completed compress tool
- `test_message_has_compress_false_no_tool` — assistant without compress
- `test_message_has_compress_false_user` — user message returns false
- `test_is_ignored_no_parts` — empty parts → ignored
- `test_is_ignored_all_parts_ignored` — all ignored → ignored
- `test_is_not_ignored_has_active_parts` — has active parts → not ignored
- `test_is_protected_user_message` — config enabled + non-ignored user → true
- `test_is_not_protected_when_disabled` — config disabled → false

## Dependencies
- dcp-messages scaffold (bead 7jq) must exist
- dcp-types for Message, Part, Role types
- dcp-config for Config type

## Why this matters
These functions are called by virtually every other module in dcp-messages (inject, sync, inject_utils, priority). Getting `is_ignored_user_message` wrong would cascade into broken nudge injection and message ID assignment.

title: dcp-compress: range mode, message mode, block bookkeeping
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
LLM-driven compression is the second layer of defense. When the model decides it needs to compress conversation history, it calls the compress tool. The library then replaces the specified range with a compact summary block. This is the most powerful compression because the model itself decides what to keep.

**Two Modes (from SPEC.md Section 6):**

Range Mode:
- Model specifies startId, endId, summary
- startId and endId are message references (m####) or block refs (b#)
- Multiple non-overlapping ranges in one call
- Each range becomes one block under one run_id

Message Mode:
- Model specifies individual messageId + topic + summary
- Each entry becomes one single-message block
- No nesting/consumption (different from range)

**Block Bookkeeping (Section 6.3):**
- BlockId: monotonic, never reused
- RunId: groups blocks from same compress call
- anchor_message_id: first non-consumed message in range
- consumed_block_ids: blocks replaced by this compression
- included_block_ids: consumed + transitive
- effective_message_ids: union of direct + consumed effective
- active/inactive: tracks whether block is current

**Nesting and Consumption (Section 6.4):**
- New block covering existing active block consumes it
- Consumed block: active=false, deactivated_at, deactivated_by_block_id
- New block records consumed_block_ids
- Consumed block records parent_block_ids

**Frontier Mechanism (Section 6.5):**
- Prevents re-compressing same range
- Advances when summary_tokens >= compressed_tokens
- Suppresses nudges for ranges at-or-before frontier
- Reset on compaction or explicit command

**filter_compressed_ranges:**
- Renders active blocks into outgoing messages
- Anchor message gets summary text
- Other messages in range removed

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-compress/
- Deps: dcp-state, dcp-traits, dcp-tokens, dcp-prompts
- Dependents: dcp-core

**Tests:**
- T-CO-1 through T-CO-12
- T-FR-1 through T-FR-3
- T-EX-1 (nesting)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Range and message modes both work
- Non-overlapping ranges validated
- Nesting/consumption works correctly
- Frontier mechanism prevents retry loops
close_reason: All implemented: cargo check passes, all tests green

title: Phase F3: Implement prompt extensions — tool.rs (compress format)
dcp-nudges: context-limit, turn, iteration nudge injection
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
Nudges are the third layer — they actively prompt the model to compress when the context is getting large. There are three kinds: context-limit, turn, and iteration. Each has its own trigger condition and is rendered via a template.

**Three Nudge Types (from SPEC.md Section 8):**

1. Context-Limit Nudge (Section 8.1):
   - Trigger: total_tokens > max_context_limit threshold
   - Frequency: every nudgeFrequency calls (not every call)
   - Anchor: most recent assistant message
   - Template placeholders: tokens, limit
   - Example: 'The conversation has reached {tokens} tokens, near the configured limit of {limit}. Consider calling the compress tool...'

2. Turn Nudge (Section 8.2):
   - Trigger: turn ended, no compression happened, no active block on pair
   - Suppressed when nudge_force = strong (iteration preferred)
   - Anchor: assistant message at turn end
   - Emitted once per pair (tracked in turn_nudged_pairs)

3. Iteration Nudge (Section 8.3):
   - Trigger: count of assistant messages since last user message > iterationNudgeThreshold
   - Re-fires every nudgeFrequency messages until new user message
   - Anchor: most recent assistant message
   - Template placeholders: count

**Priority (Section 8.4):**
Only one nudge emitted per transform. Priority: context-limit > iteration > turn.

**Injection (Section 8):**
- injection_mode: append_text or wrap_block (default)
- Appended to end of target message text
- Separated by double newline

**Frontier Suppression:**
Candidates at-or-before frontier_message_ref are dropped.

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-nudges/
- Deps: dcp-state, dcp-traits
- Dependents: dcp-core

**Tests:**
- T-NU-1 through T-NU-9

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All 3 nudge types fire correctly
- Priority system works
- Frontier suppression works
- Templates overridable via Prompts
close_reason: All implemented: cargo check passes, all tests green

title: Phase C3: Implement dcp-messages sync.rs — Compression block synchronization
description:
# Phase C3: Implement dcp-messages sync.rs — Compression block synchronization

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/sync.ts`. The core consistency checker that ensures compression blocks (ranges of messages compressed into summaries) are correctly activated, deactivated, and cross-referenced against the current message list. This is called every turn to reconcile state.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/sync.rs`

## Public API
```rust
/// Reconciles block state against current messages:
/// 1. Build Set of current message IDs
/// 2. Clear active_block_ids and active_by_anchor_message_id
/// 3. Sort blocks by created_at + block_id for deterministic processing
/// 4. For each block:
///    - If origin message (compressMessageId) missing → deactivate
///    - If deactivatedByUser → deactivate
///    - For each consumed block ID → deactivate consumed (set deactivatedByBlockId)
///    - Otherwise → activate and update anchor mapping
/// 5. Rebuild per-message active_block_ids from global active set
pub fn sync_compression_blocks(
    state: &mut SessionState,
    messages: &[Message],
);
```

## Algorithm detail
- Block consumption is transitive: if block B consumes block A, then A gets deactivated
- `deactivated_by_block_id` tracks WHICH block caused deactivation
- Origin message disappearance (pruned from context) triggers block deactivation
- Per-message `active_block_ids` is deduplicated

## Tests
- `test_sync_activates_valid_blocks` — blocks with present origin messages → active
- `test_sync_deactivates_missing_origin` — origin pruned → deactivated
- `test_sync_deactivates_by_user` — deactivatedByUser flag respected
- `test_sync_consumed_blocks_deactivated` — consumed block → deactivated with pointer
- `test_sync_rebuilds_per_message_mapping` — per-message active_block_ids correct
- `test_sync_empty_messages_deactivates_all` — no messages → all blocks deactivated
- `test_sync_deterministic_ordering` — same input → same result regardless of insertion order

## Dependencies
- dcp-messages scaffold (bead 7jq)
- dcp-state for SessionState with prune.messages fields
- dcp-types for Message, CompressionBlock

## Why this matters
Without sync, stale compression blocks (referencing pruned messages) would cause errors when the compress tool tries to resolve boundary IDs. This is the consistency guarantee that makes the system crash-safe — any state drift is corrected on the next turn.

title: dcp-protected: glob matcher (via globset), is_tool_protected, is_path_protected
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
Protected patterns prevent certain tools or file paths from being pruned. This is critical for safety — you never want to accidentally compress or prune a task creation, skill invocation, or critical configuration file. The protection is checked via glob matching (globset crate).

**Functionality:**

1. Glob matching (via globset crate):
   - compile(pattern) -> Glob
   - matches(&glob, path) -> bool
   - Supports: **, *, ?, character classes

2. Path protection check:
   - is_path_protected(path: &str, patterns: &[Glob]) -> bool
   - Used in: dedup strategy, stale_file_reads strategy

3. Tool protection check:
   - is_tool_protected(tool: &str, protected_tools: &[String]) -> bool
   - Used in: all 3 strategies (dedup, purge_errors, stale_file_reads)

4. Protected file patterns (config):
   - From config.protected_file_patterns
   - Default: ["**/*.config.ts", "Cargo.toml"]

**Glob Pattern Examples:**
- "*.config.ts" — any config.ts file
- "**/*.rs" — any Rust file in any directory
- "Cargo.toml" — specific file

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-protected/
- Deps: dcp-types (for path strings)
- Dependents: dcp-prune (strategies use it)

**Tests:**
- 50+ glob pattern test cases
- Coverage: exact match, wildcards, character classes, nested dirs

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- globset crate used (battle-tested, used by ignore crate)
- Protected paths never pruned
- Protected tools never pruned
close_reason: Implemented and verified: cargo check passes, all tests green

title: Phase F2: Implement prompt extensions — system.rs
description:
# Phase F2: Implement prompt extensions — system.rs

## Background
Port of `lib/prompts/extensions/system.ts`. Defines system-prompt extensions for special modes (manual mode, subagent mode, protected tools). These are static string constants wrapped in `<dcp-system-reminder>` XML blocks.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-prompts/src/extensions/system.rs` (new file)

## Public API
```rust
/// XML block: only compress when seeing <compress triggered manually>
/// Enforces single compress per trigger, no further task execution after manual compress
pub const MANUAL_MODE_SYSTEM_EXTENSION: &str;

/// XML block: explains subagent mode — initial instruction has no message ID, not compressible
pub const SUBAGENT_SYSTEM_EXTENSION: &str;

/// If list non-empty, returns XML block listing protected tools and instructing
/// the model not to include their content in compression summaries
pub fn build_protected_tools_extension(protected_tools: &[String]) -> String;
```

## Implementation
- Two const strings wrapped in `<dcp-system-reminder>...</dcp-system-reminder>`
- `build_protected_tools_extension`: if empty → return empty string; else format as backtick-wrapped comma-separated list inside XML block

## Tests
- `test_manual_mode_extension_format` — contains expected XML tags and instructions
- `test_subagent_extension_format` — contains subagent mode explanation
- `test_protected_tools_empty` — returns empty string
- `test_protected_tools_with_items` — returns formatted XML with tool names

## Dependencies
- None — purely string constants and simple builder function

## Why this matters
These extensions modify the system prompt to change model behavior in special modes. Without them, manual mode doesn't restrict compression triggers, subagent mode doesn't handle the prompt message correctly, and protected tool content might leak into summaries.

description:
# Phase G: Implement dcp-notification crate — Formatting & notification building

## Background
Port of `lib/ui/notification.ts` + `lib/ui/utils.ts`. Builds user-facing notifications for pruning and compression events. Unlike the TS version which sends via client.tui.showToast() or sendIgnoredMessage(), the Rust version produces formatted strings and lets the host decide delivery.

## Target files
- `crates/dcp-notification/src/format.rs`
- `crates/dcp-notification/src/notification.rs`
- `crates/dcp-notification/src/lib.rs`

## format.rs — Port of lib/ui/utils.ts
```rust
pub fn format_stats_header(total_tokens_saved: u64, prune_token_counter: u64) -> String;
pub fn format_token_count(tokens: u64, compact: bool) -> String;  // 1000+ → "X.XK"
pub fn truncate(s: &str, max_len: usize) -> String;
pub fn format_progress_bar(message_ids: &[String], pruned: &HashSet<String>, recent: &[String], width: usize) -> String;
pub fn cache_system_prompt_tokens(state: &mut SessionState, messages: &[Message]);
pub fn shorten_path(input: &str, working_directory: Option<&str>) -> String;
pub fn format_pruned_items_list(ids: &[String], meta: &HashMap<String, ToolParameterEntry>, wd: Option<&str>) -> Vec<String>;
pub fn format_pruning_result_for_tool(ids: &[String], meta: &HashMap<String, ToolParameterEntry>, wd: Option<&str>) -> String;

// Internal — large match on tool types:
fn extract_parameter_key(tool: &str, params: &serde_json::Value) -> String;
```

## notification.rs — Port of lib/ui/notification.ts
```rust
pub type PruneReason = &'static str; // "completion" | "noise" | "extraction"

pub struct CompressionNotificationEntry {
    pub block_id: u32, pub run_id: u32,
    pub summary: String, pub summary_tokens: u64,
}

pub fn send_unified_notification(
    state: &SessionState, config: &Config,
    prune_tool_ids: &[String], tool_metadata: &HashMap<String, ToolParameterEntry>,
    reason: PruneReason, working_directory: Option<&str>,
) -> String;

pub fn send_compress_notification(
    state: &SessionState, config: &Config,
    entries: &[CompressionNotificationEntry],
    batch_topic: Option<&str>, session_message_ids: &[String],
) -> String;
```

## Tests
- `test_format_stats_header` — correct format "▣ DCP | ~XK saved total"
- `test_format_token_count_large` — 1500 → "1.5K tokens"
- `test_format_token_count_small` — 500 → "500 tokens"
- `test_format_token_count_compact` — no " tokens" suffix
- `test_truncate_short` — returns unchanged if under limit
- `test_truncate_long` — appends "..."
- `test_progress_bar_rendering` — correct Unicode block characters
- `test_shorten_path_with_wd` — strips working directory prefix
- `test_shorten_path_without_wd` — returns unchanged
- `test_format_pruned_items_list` — correct "→ tool: param" format
- `test_extract_parameter_key_read` — file path with line range
- `test_extract_parameter_key_bash` — description or truncated command
- `test_extract_parameter_key_unknown` — first 50 chars of JSON
- `test_unified_notification_minimal` — minimal mode format
- `test_unified_notification_detailed` — detailed mode with item list
- `test_compress_notification_single` — single block notification
- `test_compress_notification_batch` — multiple blocks notification

## Dependencies
- dcp-notification scaffold (bead mwr)
- dcp-state for SessionState, Stats
- dcp-types for Message, Part, ToolParameterEntry

## Why this matters
Notifications are how users know DCP is working. Without formatted output, users have no visibility into what was pruned, how many tokens were saved, or whether compression succeeded. The progress bar provides a visual map of the message stream.

title: Phase C4: Implement dcp-messages priority.rs — Message priority classification
description:
# Phase C4: Implement dcp-messages priority.rs — Message priority classification

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/priority.ts`. Classifies messages by token count priority (high/medium/low) and provides utilities for listing priority message references before an anchor point. Used by the nudge injection system to tell the model which messages to compress first.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/priority.rs`

## Types
```rust
pub type MessagePriority = &'static str; // "low" | "medium" | "high"

pub struct CompressionPriorityEntry {
    pub ref_id: String,
    pub token_count: usize,
    pub priority: MessagePriority,
}

pub type CompressionPriorityMap = HashMap<String, CompressionPriorityEntry>;
```

## Public API
```rust
/// Builds priority map for all messages (only in "message" mode)
pub fn build_priority_map(config: &Config, state: &SessionState, messages: &[Message]) -> CompressionPriorityMap;

/// Thresholds: 5000+ = high, 500+ = medium, else low
pub fn classify_message_priority(token_count: usize) -> MessagePriority;

/// Returns deduplicated refs of messages with given priority that appear before anchor_index
pub fn list_priority_refs_before_index(
    messages: &[Message], priorities: &CompressionPriorityMap,
    anchor_index: usize, priority: MessagePriority,
) -> Vec<String>;
```

## Tests
- `test_classify_high` — 5000+ tokens → high
- `test_classify_medium` — 500-4999 tokens → medium
- `test_classify_low` — <500 tokens → low
- `test_build_priority_map_message_mode` — builds map in message mode
- `test_build_priority_map_range_mode_empty` — returns empty in range mode
- `test_list_refs_before_index` — returns correct refs before anchor
- `test_list_refs_deduplication` — no duplicates in output

## Dependencies
- dcp-messages scaffold (bead 7jq)
- dcp-state for message ID mappings

## Why this matters
Priority classification lets the nudge system say "these high-token messages are consuming context — compress them first". Without it, the model has no guidance on what to prioritize.

description:
# Phase C7: Implement dcp-messages inject.rs — Nudge & message ID injection

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/inject/inject.ts`. The main injection orchestrator handling two concerns: (a) injecting nudge text into messages to encourage compression, and (b) injecting message ID tags so the system can track messages across turns. This is the top-level entry point called by the transform pipeline.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/inject.rs`

## Public API
```rust
/// Primary nudge injection driver. Implements 3-tier nudge system:
/// - Tier 1 (overMaxLimit): adds to contextLimitAnchors
/// - Tier 2 (overMinLimit): adds to turnNudgeAnchors + iterationNudgeAnchors
/// - Resets lower tiers when context drops below minLimit
/// - Clears all if last assistant has completed compress
pub fn inject_compress_nudges(
    state: &mut SessionState,
    config: &Config,
    messages: &mut Vec<Message>,
    prompts: &RuntimePrompts,
    priorities: Option<&CompressionPriorityMap>,
);

/// Injects <dcp-message-id> tags into message parts for identity tracking.
/// User messages: append to text part or create synthetic.
/// Assistant messages: try tool parts first, then text, then synthetic.
/// Blocked messages get "BLOCKED" tag variant.
pub fn inject_message_ids(
    state: &mut SessionState,
    config: &Config,
    messages: &mut Vec<Message>,
    priorities: Option<&CompressionPriorityMap>,
);
```

## Nudge injection flow
1. Permission check via `compress_permission()` — returns early if denied
2. Manual mode check — skips nudges entirely
3. Check context limits via `is_context_over_limits()`
4. Manage 3 anchor sets: contextLimit, turn, iteration
5. Delegate to `apply_anchored_nudges()` for actual text injection

## Message ID injection flow
1. For each message, get its ref from state
2. Generate `<dcp-message-id ...>mNNNN</dcp-message-id>` tag
3. Inject into appropriate part based on message role

## Tests
- `test_inject_nudges_tier1_over_max` — context limit anchors added
- `test_inject_nudges_tier2_over_min` — turn + iteration anchors added
- `test_inject_nudges_resets_when_below_min` — anchors cleared
- `test_inject_nudges_clears_on_compress` — all anchors cleared after compress
- `test_inject_nudges_skips_in_manual_mode` — no injection in manual mode
- `test_inject_nudges_skips_when_denied` — no injection when permission denied
- `test_inject_message_ids_user_message` — tag appended to user message
- `test_inject_message_ids_assistant_message` — tag appended to assistant
- `test_inject_message_ids_blocked_message` — BLOCKED variant used
- `test_inject_message_ids_idempotent` — running twice doesn't duplicate tags

## Dependencies
- Phase C1 (query.rs) — message_has_compress, is_ignored_user_message
- Phase C6 (inject_utils.rs) — is_context_over_limits, apply_anchored_nudges, add_anchor
- Phase C5 (utils.rs) — append_to_last_text_part, create_synthetic_text_part
- Phase A3 (compress_permission) — compress_permission check

## Why this matters
This is the entry point for the entire nudge system. Without it, the model never gets told to compress, and message tracking doesn't work. The 3-tier system ensures proportional response — mild nudges at the lower limit, strong nudges at the upper limit.

title: Write SPEC.md - clean-room behavior specification
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
SPEC.md is the single source of truth for runtime behavior of the dynamic_context_pruning library. It is written in prose plus pseudocode, intentionally implementation-language-agnostic. The Rust crate is one valid implementation; any other implementation that satisfies every requirement, invariant, and test fixture defined here is also conformant.

This document was authored independently from public design notes and research summaries. It does NOT reference, copy, or derive structure from any prior codebase in any language. Pseudocode in this file is illustrative only and must be re-expressed by the implementer in idiomatic target-language code.

**Scope (from PLAN.md Section 13.4):**
1. Glossary — definitions for: Message, Block, Run, Anchor, Compaction, Prune, Frontier
2. Canonical IR — Message/Part shape, role semantics, ID rules
3. Session lifecycle — start, turn, compaction, end
4. Tool tracking — call/result pairing, status transitions
5. Strategies:
   - 5.1 Deduplicate (full algorithm pseudocode)
   - 5.2 Purge errors
   - 5.3 Stale file reads
6. Compression:
   - 6.1 Range mode protocol
   - 6.2 Message mode protocol
   - 6.3 Block bookkeeping rules
   - 6.4 Nesting & consumption
   - 6.5 Frontier mechanism
7. Cache stability:
   - 7.1 Mode definitions
   - 7.2 Pending state semantics
   - 7.3 Apply triggers
8. Nudges:
   - 8.1 Context-limit
   - 8.2 Turn
   - 8.3 Iteration
9. Persistence:
   - 9.1 Schema V1
   - 9.2 Migration rules
   - 9.3 Atomic write protocol
10. Configuration:
   - 10.1 Cascade order
   - 10.2 Field semantics
   - 10.3 Validation rules
11. Edge cases & invariants:
   - 11.1 Tool call/result pairing must be preserved
   - 11.2 Block IDs monotonic
   - 11.3 UTF-8 boundary safety
   - 11.4 Idempotent rebuild guarantee
12. Test fixtures — minimum coverage matrix

**Justification:**
The spec is essential because it allows clean-room implementation. Without it, implementers might accidentally derive from upstream AGPL code. The spec ensures MIT licensing is clean.

**Dependencies:**
This bead should be created before any implementation begins. It blocks all Phase 1+ beads.

**Deliverables:**
- SPEC.md file at repo root, ~2500 lines
- Clean prose, no pseudocode that could be mistaken for actual Rust
- 60+ test fixtures covering all sections
- No upstream code references

# Phase A3: Implement compress_permission.rs — Compress Permission Adapter

## Background
Port of `lib/compress-permission.ts`. Thin adapter layer bridging the config system, host-permissions engine, and session state. Keeps the compress permission in sync with host-level overrides.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-permissions/src/compress_permission.rs`

## Public API
```rust
/// Returns state.compress_permission if set, else falls back to config.compress.permission
pub fn compress_permission(state: &SessionState, config: &Config) -> PermissionAction;

/// Resolves effective compress permission and stores in state:
/// 1. Find last user message → extract agent name
/// 2. Call resolve_effective_compress_permission with base + host + agent
/// 3. Store result in state.compress_permission
pub fn sync_compress_permission_state(
    state: &mut SessionState,
    config: &Config,
    host_permissions: &HostPermissionSnapshot,
    messages: &[Message],
);
```

## Implementation
- `compress_permission()`: match on `state.compress_permission` first, if Some return it, else return `config.compress.permission`
- `sync_compress_permission_state()`: find last non-ignored user message, extract `.info.agent`, call `resolve_effective_compress_permission(config.compress.permission, host_permissions, agent_name)`, store result

## Tests
- `test_compress_permission_uses_state_when_set` — returns state value when set
- `test_compress_permission_falls_back_to_config` — returns config value when state is None
- `test_sync_extracts_agent_from_last_user_message` — agent name from last user msg
- `test_sync_stores_resolved_permission_in_state` — verify state is updated
- `test_sync_with_no_host_permissions` — returns base config permission

## Dependencies
- Phase A2 (host_permissions.rs) must be complete — this bead calls `resolve_effective_compress_permission`
- Requires SessionState to have `compress_permission: Option<PermissionAction>` field
- Requires Config to have `compress.permission: PermissionAction` field

## Why this matters
This is called during every session lifecycle to keep compress permission in sync with host-level overrides. Without it, the compress tool might run when the host has explicitly denied it.

title: dcp-tokens: char/4 default, tokenizers feature, tiktoken-fast feature
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
Tokenizers convert strings to token counts. The library uses token counting for: (1) estimating context size for nudges, (2) measuring compression ratio for frontier, (3) tracking tokens saved by pruning strategies. The default char/4 heuristic requires zero dependencies; the feature-gated backends provide accuracy for specific providers.

**Tokenizer Implementations:**

1. CharsDiv4 (default, no deps):
   - count = text.len() / 4
   - Fast but rough estimate
   - Used when no feature flag enabled

2. HuggingFace tokenizers (feature = "tokenizers"):
   - Uses tokenizers crate
   - Accurate for any tokenizer.json
   - DEFAULT when feature enabled

3. Tiktoken (feature = "tiktoken-fast"):
   - Uses tiktoken crate (lib.rs, not tiktoken-rs)
   - 7-10x faster than tiktoken-rs
   - Best for OpenAI models

4. Claude tokenizer (feature = "claude-tokens"):
   - Uses claude-tokenizer crate
   - Accurate for Claude models
   - Most accurate for Anthropic

**TokenCounter trait:**
- fn count(&self, text: &str) -> usize

**Image handling:**
- Image tokens = tokenizer.image_tokens (default 1500)
- Per SPEC.md Section 10.2 field "tokenizer.imageTokens"

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-tokens/
- Deps: dcp-types, dcp-traits (for Tokenizer trait)
- Dependents: dcp-core, dcp-compress, dcp-nudges

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All 4 tokenizer implementations work
- Char/4 works with zero deps
- Feature flags correctly include only requested impl

title: Phase C6: Implement dcp-messages inject_utils.rs — Anchor management & limit checking
description:
# Phase C6: Implement dcp-messages inject_utils.rs — Anchor management & limit checking

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/inject/utils.ts`. The utility layer for the injection system — manages nudge frequency, iteration thresholds, context limit checking, anchor management, and the actual nudge text application. This is the most algorithmically complex file in the messages module.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/inject_utils.rs`

## Types
```rust
pub struct LastUserModelContext {
    pub provider_id: Option<String>,
    pub model_id: Option<String>,
}
```

## Public API
```rust
pub fn get_nudge_frequency(config: &Config) -> usize;       // Math.max(1, floor(nudgeFrequency))
pub fn get_iteration_nudge_threshold(config: &Config) -> usize; // Math.max(1, floor(iterationNudgeThreshold))
pub fn find_last_non_ignored_message(messages: &[Message]) -> Option<(usize, &Message)>;
pub fn count_messages_after_index(messages: &[Message], index: usize) -> usize;
pub fn get_model_info(messages: &[Message]) -> LastUserModelContext;

/// Resolves token limits (absolute or percentage), compares against current usage
/// Returns (over_max_limit, over_min_limit)
pub fn is_context_over_limits(
    config: &Config, state: &SessionState,
    provider_id: Option<&str>, model_id: Option<&str>,
    messages: &[Message],
) -> (bool, bool);

/// Adds anchor only if distance from last existing anchor >= interval
pub fn add_anchor(
    anchor_set: &mut HashSet<String>,
    message_id: &str,
    message_index: usize,
    messages: &[Message],
    interval: usize,
) -> bool;

/// The workhorse that actually injects nudge text into anchored messages
/// Two modes: message mode (per-message priority guidance) and range mode (compressed block guidance)
pub fn apply_anchored_nudges(
    state: &mut SessionState,
    config: &Config,
    messages: &mut Vec<Message>,
    prompts: &RuntimePrompts,
    priorities: Option<&CompressionPriorityMap>,
);
```

## Context limit resolution
- Per-model limit (keyed by "provider/model") > global limit > undefined (no limit)
- Supports absolute numbers AND percentage strings ("80%")
- Percentage: clamp to [0, 100], multiply by `state.model_context_limit`
- Summary buffer: add to max limit when `config.compress.summary_buffer` is true

## Nudge application modes
- **Message mode**: calls `applyMessageModeAnchoredNudge` — appends per-message priority guidance listing high-priority refs before anchor
- **Range mode**: calls `applyRangeModeAnchoredNudge` — appends compressed block guidance listing active blocks

## Tests
- `test_nudge_frequency_minimum_one` — never returns 0
- `test_iteration_threshold_minimum_one` — never returns 0
- `test_find_last_non_ignored` — finds last non-ignored
- `test_find_last_all_ignored` — returns None
- `test_count_messages_after_index` — correct count
- `test_model_info_extracts_provider_model` — extracts from last user message
- `test_context_over_max_limit` — exceeds max → true
- `test_context_under_min_limit` — below min → false
- `test_context_percentage_limit` — "80%" computed correctly
- `test_per_model_limit_overrides_global` — model-specific takes precedence
- `test_add_anchor_respects_interval` — respects minimum gap
- `test_add_anchor_first_always_added` — first anchor always added
- `test_apply_anchored_nudges_message_mode` — injects priority guidance
- `test_apply_anchored_nudges_range_mode` — injects block guidance

## Dependencies
- Phase C1 (query.rs) — uses is_ignored_user_message, get_last_user_message
- Phase C4 (priority.rs) — uses CompressionPriorityMap
- Phase C5 (utils.rs) — uses append_to_last_text_part
- Phase F1 (nudge.rs) — uses build_compressed_block_guidance, render_message_priority_guidance
- dcp-prompts for RuntimePrompts

## Why this matters
This is the brain of the nudge system. It decides WHEN to nudge (context limit checks), WHERE to nudge (anchor placement), and WHAT to say (priority guidance vs block guidance). Getting the limit resolution wrong means either over-nudging (wasting tokens) or under-nudging (hitting context limits).

title: Phase C5: Implement dcp-messages utils.rs — Synthetic messages & hallucination strip
description:
# Phase C5: Implement dcp-messages utils.rs — Synthetic messages & hallucination strip

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/utils.ts`. Utility functions for creating synthetic messages (used to inject summary blocks), appending text to message parts, building tool ID lists, and stripping DCP tag hallucinations. This is the workhorse utility module.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/utils.rs`

## Public API
```rust
// Synthetic message creation (for compression summary injection)
pub fn create_synthetic_user_message(base: &Message, content: &str, stable_seed: Option<&str>) -> Message;
pub fn create_synthetic_text_part(base: &Message, content: &str, stable_seed: Option<&str>) -> Part;

// Text injection
pub fn append_to_last_text_part(message: &mut Message, injection: &str) -> bool;
pub fn append_to_all_tool_parts(message: &mut Message, tag: &str) -> bool;
pub fn has_content(message: &Message) -> bool;

// Tool ID tracking
pub fn build_tool_id_list(state: &SessionState, messages: &[Message]) -> Vec<String>;

// Hallucination prevention
pub fn replace_block_ids_with_blocked(text: &str) -> String;
pub fn strip_hallucinations_from_string(text: &str) -> String;
pub fn strip_hallucinations(messages: &mut Vec<Message>);
```

## ID generation
Uses SHA256 truncated to 16 hex chars with deterministic prefixes:
- `msg_dcp_summary_<hex16>` for synthetic messages
- `prt_dcp_summary_<hex16>` for synthetic parts
- `prt_dcp_text_<hex16>` for synthetic text parts
- Uses `sha2` crate for hashing

## Regex constants for DCP tags
- `DCP_BLOCK_ID_TAG_REGEX`: matches `<dcp-block-id ...>...</dcp-block-id>`
- `DCP_PAIRED_TAG_REGEX`: matches any `<dcp-* ...>...</dcp-*>` paired tags
- `DCP_UNPAIRED_TAG_REGEX`: matches self-closing `<dcp-*/>` tags

## Tests
- `test_create_synthetic_user_message_deterministic_id` — same seed → same ID
- `test_create_synthetic_text_part_deterministic_id` — same seed → same ID
- `test_append_to_last_text_part_success` — text appended
- `test_append_to_last_text_part_no_text` — returns false
- `test_append_to_all_tool_parts` — all tool parts get tag
- `test_has_content_with_text` — returns true
- `test_has_content_empty` — returns false
- `test_build_tool_id_list_ordered` — maintains message order
- `test_replace_block_ids_with_blocked` — b1, b2 → BLOCKED
- `test_strip_hallucinations_removes_dcp_tags` — all DCP tags removed
- `test_strip_hallucinations_from_string` — specific pattern removal

## Dependencies
- dcp-messages scaffold (bead 7jq)
- `sha2` crate for ID generation
- `regex` crate for pattern matching

## Why this matters
Synthetic messages are how compressed summaries get injected into the message stream. Hallucination stripping prevents the model from accidentally generating DCP tags in its output. Without this, compressed ranges would have no way to insert summary content.

title: Phase I: Implement CLI utility subcommands (opencode scripts port)

# Phase I: Implement CLI utility subcommands (opencode scripts port)

## Background

- `crates/dcp-cli/src/cmd/find_session.rs` — Session search by title
- `crates/dcp-cli/src/cmd/get_message.rs` — Message retrieval by ID
- `crates/dcp-cli/src/cmd/token_stats.rs` — Cross-session token aggregation
- `crates/dcp-cli/src/cmd/timeline.rs` — Per-session step-by-step timeline
- `crates/dcp-cli/src/cmd/message_tokens.rs` — Per-message token counting

## db.rs — Port of scripts/opencode_api.py
```rust
pub struct OpencodeDb { conn: rusqlite::Connection }

impl OpencodeDb {
    pub fn open(path: Option<&str>) -> Result<Self>;  // ~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db, ?mode=ro
    pub fn list_projects(&self) -> Result<Vec<ProjectRow>>;
    pub fn list_sessions(&self, directory: Option<&str>, limit: usize) -> Result<Vec<SessionRow>>;
    pub fn get_session(&self, session_id: &str) -> Result<Option<SessionRow>>;
    pub fn get_session_messages(&self, session_id: &str) -> Result<Vec<MessageRow>>;
    pub fn get_session_message(&self, session_id: &str, message_id: &str) -> Result<Option<MessageRow>>;
}
```
DB schema: project, session, message, part tables with JSON data columns.

## Subcommands (each with clap args)

### `dcp stats` — Port of opencode-dcp-stats
- Scans sessions for DCP tool calls (compress, prune, distill, etc.)
- Compares token counts before/after DCP events
- Calculates cache hit rate delta, context size delta, cost impact
- Args: --sessions N, --min-messages M, --json, --verbose, --session ID

### `dcp find-session` — Port of opencode-find-session
- Filters sessions by title (substring or exact match)
- Args: search-term, --exact, --json, --all

### `dcp get-message` — Port of opencode-get-message
- Retrieves full message payloads by ID
- With --session: direct lookup; without: scan recent sessions
- Args: message-id..., --session ID, --scan-sessions N

### `dcp token-stats` — Port of opencode-token-stats
- Aggregates token usage across recent sessions
- Per-session: input, output, reasoning, cache read/write, cost
- Args: --sessions N, --session ID, --json

### `dcp timeline` — Port of opencode-session-timeline
- Step-by-step token timeline for a session
- Shows cache growth/decay, DCP tool usage, durations
- Color-coded cache hit rate (>=80% green, >=50% yellow, <50% red)
- Args: --session ID, --json, --no-color

### `dcp message-tokens` — Port of opencode-message-token-counts
- Per-message token counts for a session
- Uses tokenizer to count (or falls back to len/4)
- Renders table with token counts, size bars, highlights 5 largest
- Args: --session ID, --json, --no-color

## New dependency
- `rusqlite = { version = "0.31", features = ["bundled"] }` — optional, feature-gated as `scripts`

## Tests
- `test_db_open_default_path` — opens default DB path
- `test_db_open_custom_path` — opens specified path
- `test_db_list_projects` — returns project rows
- `test_stats_empty_sessions` — handles no sessions gracefully
- `test_find_session_by_title` — finds matching sessions
- `test_find_session_exact_match` — exact match mode
- `test_token_stats_aggregation` — correct totals
- `test_timeline_step_rendering` — correct step output
- `test_message_tokens_counting` — accurate token counts
- Integration: create temp SQLite DB with test data, run each command

## Dependencies
- dcp-cli crate (already exists)
- All data types from dcp-types, dcp-state, dcp-messages
- rusqlite for SQLite access

## Why this matters
These scripts are the observability layer. Without them, users cannot measure DCP's impact on cache efficiency, find specific sessions, or debug compression issues. They're essential for both development and production monitoring.

title: dcp-types: canonical IR (Message, Part, BlockId, RunId, MessageRef, core types)
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
dcp-types defines the canonical Internal Representation (IR) — the common language all parts of the library speak. Every component, from dcp-prune to dcp-compress to dcp-core, depends on these types. If the IR changes, everything changes. This is the foundation of the entire architecture.

**Canonical IR Elements (from SPEC.md Section 2):**

Message and Part types:
- Message: id, role, parts, time
- Part: Text(String), Reasoning(String), ToolCall, ToolResult, Image
- Role: User, Assistant, System
- ToolStatus: Pending, Running, Completed, Error

Identifier types:
- BlockId (u32, monotonic)
- RunId (u32, monotonic)
- MessageRef (String, m#### format)
- RawId (opaque host-assigned)

Compression types:
- CompressionBlock: block_id, run_id, anchor, mode, summary, consumed_ids, etc.
- CompressionMode: Range, Message

State types:
- SessionState: session_id, current_turn, prune, nudges, stats, etc.
- Prune: tools map, messages state
- Stats: all telemetry counters

**Implementation Notes:**
- All types must be serde-serializable for persistence
- No internal dependencies (dcp-types is the leaf)
- Chrono for timestamps (serde-compatible)
- Serde_json for JSON value handling

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-types/
- Deps: workspace only (no internal deps)
- Dependents: ALL other crates

**Tests (from SPEC.md Section 12):**
- T-IR-1 through T-IR-5: Canonical IR validation
- T-TT-1 through T-TT-7: Tool tracking
- All invariants: 11.1-11.10

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All types have serde derive
- Message validation per Section 2.5
- Reference allocation per Section 2.4
- No uncommented public fields

title: Phase F4: Implement PromptStore with 3-tier override cascade
description:
# Phase F4: Implement PromptStore with 3-tier override cascade

## Background
Port of `lib/prompts/store.ts`. Manages the complete lifecycle of DCP prompt templates: bundled defaults, three-tier override system, normalization, XML wrapping, and persistence of default files to disk. This is the most complex piece of the prompts module.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-prompts/src/store.rs` (new file)

## Types
```rust
pub type PromptKey = &'static str;
// "system" | "compress-range" | "compress-message" | "context-limit-nudge" | "turn-nudge" | "iteration-nudge"

pub struct RuntimePrompts {
    pub system: String,
    pub compress_range: String,
    pub compress_message: String,
    pub context_limit_nudge: String,
    pub turn_nudge: String,
    pub iteration_nudge: String,
    pub manual_extension: String,
    pub subagent_extension: String,
}

pub const PROMPT_KEYS: &[PromptKey] = &["system", "compress-range", "compress-message", "context-limit-nudge", "turn-nudge", "iteration-nudge"];
```

## Public API
```rust
pub struct PromptStore { /* internal */ }

impl PromptStore {
    pub fn new(working_directory: &Path, custom_prompts_enabled: bool) -> Self;
    pub fn get_runtime_prompts(&self) -> RuntimePrompts;
    pub fn reload(&mut self);
}
```

## Override cascade (highest priority first)
1. **Project**: `.opencode/dcp-prompts/overrides/<filename>` — walk up from working_directory to find `.opencode/`
2. **Config dir**: `$OPENCODE_CONFIG_DIR/dcp-prompts/overrides/<filename>`
3. **Global**: `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/opencode/dcp-prompts/overrides/<filename>` (default: `~/.config/opencode/`)

## Normalization pipeline
1. Strip BOM marker
2. Normalize line endings (CRLF → LF)
3. Remove HTML comments `<!-- ... -->`
4. Remove legacy `// ... //` inline comment lines
5. For system prompt: strip `<manual>...</manual>` and `<subagent>...</subagent>` blocks (handled as extensions)
6. For non-compress prompts: unwrap outer `<dcp-system-reminder>...</dcp-system-reminder>` for editing
7. Validate opening/closing tags are balanced

## Runtime wrapping
- Non-compress prompts: re-wrap in `<dcp-system-reminder>...</dcp-system-reminder>`
- Compress prompts (compress-range, compress-message): NOT wrapped (raw text)

## ensure_default_files()
- Creates `defaults/` and `overrides/` directories under `~/.config/opencode/dcp-prompts/`
- Writes each prompt's editable text as a `.md` file
- Writes a `README.md` with usage instructions
- Only writes if content differs (idempotent)

## Prompt definition table (6 entries)
| Key | File | Runtime Field |
|---|---|---|
| system | system.md | system |
| compress-range | compress-range.md | compressRange |
| compress-message | compress-message.md | compressMessage |
| context-limit-nudge | context-limit-nudge.md | contextLimitNudge |
| turn-nudge | turn-nudge.md | turnNudge |
| iteration-nudge | iteration-nudge.md | iterationNudge |

## Tests
- `test_default_prompts_load` — all 6 prompts load from bundled defaults
- `test_override_project_level` — project override takes precedence
- `test_override_config_dir` — config dir override works
- `test_override_global_level` — global override works
- `test_cascade_priority` — project > config > global
- `test_normalization_strips_comments` — HTML and inline comments removed
- `test_normalization_strips_bom` — BOM marker removed
- `test_normalization_unwraps_dcp_tags` — outer tag stripped for editing
- `test_rerap_at_runtime` — non-compress prompts get wrapped
- `test_compress_prompts_not_wrapped` — compress prompts stay raw
- `test_ensure_default_files_idempotent` — running twice produces same result
- `test_reload_picks_up_changes` — modify override file, reload sees changes

## Dependencies
- Phase F1 (nudge.rs), F2 (system.rs), F3 (tool.rs) should be done for manual_extension and subagent_extension
- Uses `dirs` crate for XDG config home (already in workspace deps)

## Why this matters
Without PromptStore, users cannot customize prompt templates. The 3-tier cascade allows project-level, user-level, and global defaults. The normalization pipeline ensures that user-written overrides work even if they include extra whitespace, comments, or wrapping.

title: Phase B: Extend dcp-state message_ids.rs with full TS parity
description:
# Phase B: Extend dcp-state message_ids.rs with full TS parity

## Background
Port of `lib/message-ids.ts`. The existing `crates/dcp-state/src/message_refs.rs` has basic ref formatting but is missing: XML tag generation, ref allocation with capacity limits, boundary ID parsing, and the full assign_message_refs walk. These are essential for the compress tool to work (the model uses m0001/b1 style refs to specify compression ranges).

## Target file
`crates/dcp-state/src/message_ids.rs` (rename message_refs.rs → message_ids.rs, or extend in place)

## Additions needed

### Constants
```rust
pub const MESSAGE_REF_WIDTH: usize = 4;
pub const MESSAGE_REF_MIN_INDEX: u16 = 1;
pub const MESSAGE_REF_MAX_INDEX: u16 = 9999;
```

### Types
```rust
pub enum ParsedBoundaryId {
    Message { ref_id: String, index: u16 },
    CompressedBlock { ref_id: String, block_id: u32 },
}
```

### Functions to add
```rust
// Already existing (verify):
pub fn format_message_ref(index: u16) -> String;  // "m0001"
pub fn format_block_ref(block_id: u32) -> String;  // "b1"

// New:
pub fn parse_message_ref(s: &str) -> Option<u16>;
pub fn parse_block_ref(s: &str) -> Option<u32>;
pub fn parse_boundary_id(id: &str) -> Option<ParsedBoundaryId>;
pub fn format_message_id_tag(ref_id: &str, attributes: Option<&HashMap<String, String>>) -> String;
pub fn assign_message_refs(state: &mut SessionState, messages: &[Message]) -> usize;

// Internal:
fn escape_xml_attribute(value: &str) -> String;  // & " < > → & " < >
fn allocate_next_message_ref(state: &mut SessionState) -> Result<String, Error>;
```

## Key algorithms
- `format_message_id_tag`: generate `\n<dcp-message-id attr1="val1">ref</dcp-message-id>` with sorted attributes and XML escaping
- `assign_message_refs`: walk messages, skip ignored users, skip first subagent prompt, allocate refs sequentially, maintain bidirectional mapping (by_raw_id + by_ref)
- `allocate_next_message_ref`: linear scan from next_ref, throw error at 9999

## Tests
- `test_format_message_ref_zero_padded` — 1 → "m0001"
- `test_format_message_ref_max` — 9999 → "m9999"
- `test_format_block_ref` — 1 → "b1", 42 → "b42"
- `test_parse_message_ref_valid` — "m0001" → Some(1)
- `test_parse_message_ref_invalid` — "x0001" → None
- `test_parse_block_ref_valid` — "b42" → Some(42)
- `test_parse_boundary_id_message` — "m0001" → Message variant
- `test_parse_boundary_id_block` — "b5" → CompressedBlock variant
- `test_format_message_id_tag_simple` — no attributes
- `test_format_message_id_tag_with_attributes` — attributes sorted, XML escaped
- `test_assign_message_refs_sequential` — refs assigned in order
- `test_assign_message_refs_skips_ignored` — ignored messages skipped
- `test_assign_message_refs_idempotent` — running twice doesn't reassign
- `test_allocate_next_message_ref_capacity` — error at 9999

## Dependencies
- Phase C1 (query.rs is_ignored_user_message) — needed for skipping ignored messages
- dcp-state crate (already exists)

## Why this matters
Message refs are the addressing system the model uses to specify compression ranges. Without parse_boundary_id, the compress tool cannot understand what "m0003" or "b2" means. Without assign_message_refs, new messages don't get tracked. The 9999 capacity limit prevents runaway allocation.

**Background & Purpose:**
Traits define the interfaces that allow customization and dependency injection. Every major component (tokenizer, storage, memory, cache accounting, pruning strategies) is a trait so the host can provide their own implementation or use the defaults.

**Traits (from PLAN.md Section 3.4 and SPEC.md):**

Tokenizer trait:
- fn count(&self, text: &str) -> usize
- Must be Send + Sync (can be shared across threads)

StatePersistence trait:
- fn load(&self, session_id: &str) -> Result<Option<PersistedState>>
- fn save(&self, session_id: &str, state: &PersistedState) -> Result<()>
- Must be Send + Sync

MemoryRetriever trait:
- fn retrieve(&self, query: &str, k: usize) -> Result<Vec<RetrievedMemory>>
- Used for injecting external memory into compress summaries

CacheAccountant trait:
- fn cost_per_cache_miss(&self, tokens: usize) -> f64
- fn record_event(&mut self, event: CacheEvent)
- Tracks cache hit/miss economics

PruneStrategy trait:
- fn name(&self) -> &str
- fn apply(&self, state: &mut SessionState, messages: &[Message]) -> Result<PruneOutcome>
- Plugin model for additional strategies beyond the 3 built-in

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-traits/
- Deps: dcp-types (for Message, Part, SessionState references)
- Dependents: dcp-storage, dcp-prune, dcp-core, dcp-telemetry

title: Phase E: Implement dcp-messages reasoning_strip.rs — Strip stale provider metadata
description:
# Phase E: Implement dcp-messages reasoning_strip.rs — Strip stale provider metadata

## Background
Port of `lib/messages/reasoning-strip.ts`. Strips provider-specific metadata (like reasoning_content) from assistant message parts when those parts originated from a different model/provider than the current turn's user message. This mirrors opencode's "differentModel" handling.

## Target file
`crates/dcp-messages/src/reasoning_strip.rs`

## Public API
```rust
/// Iterates all messages. For each assistant message whose model/provider differs
/// from the last user message's model, strips the metadata field from text, tool,
/// and reasoning parts.
pub fn strip_stale_metadata(messages: &mut Vec<Message>);
```

## Algorithm
1. Find last user message → extract provider/model IDs
2. If no last user message → return (nothing to compare against)
3. For each assistant message:
   - If provider or model differs from last user message's:
     - For each text/tool/reasoning part: remove the metadata field
4. Matching model+provider → metadata kept as-is

## Tests
- `test_strip_removes_mismatched_metadata` — different model → stripped
- `test_strip_keeps_matching_metadata` — same model → kept
- `test_strip_no_user_message` — no last user → nothing stripped
- `test_strip_handles_multiple_parts` — all parts with mismatched model stripped

## Dependencies
- Phase C1 (query.rs) — get_last_user_message for model info
- dcp-types for Message, Part

## Why this matters
When a user switches models mid-conversation (e.g., from Claude to GPT), the old assistant messages contain provider-specific metadata that the new provider doesn't understand. Keeping it could cause parsing errors or incorrect context rendering.

title: Phase A2: Implement host_permissions.rs — Permission Resolution Engine
description:
# Phase A2: Implement host_permissions.rs — Permission Resolution Engine

## Background
Port of `lib/host-permissions.ts`. This is the most complex file in the permissions module. It resolves tool-level permissions (ask/allow/deny) from a layered configuration system with wildcard pattern matching, last-match-wins semantics, and per-agent overrides.

The TS version supports:
- `PermissionAction` enum (ask/allow/deny)
- `PermissionValue` which is either a simple action OR a map of glob patterns → actions
- `PermissionConfig` as optional HashMap of tool name → permission value
- `HostPermissionSnapshot` with global + per-agent layers
- Wildcard matching with `*` and `?` support
- Case-insensitive on Windows, case-sensitive elsewhere

## Target file
`crates/dcp-permissions/src/host_permissions.rs`

## Types
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum PermissionAction { Ask, Allow, Deny }

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub enum PermissionValue {
    Simple(PermissionAction),
    Patterned(HashMap<String, PermissionAction>),
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct PermissionRule {
    pub permission: String,   // tool name pattern (e.g., "comp*", "*")
    pub pattern: String,      // sub-pattern (e.g., "*" for catch-all)
    pub action: PermissionAction,
}

pub struct HostPermissionSnapshot {
    pub global: Option<HashMap<String, PermissionValue>>,
    pub agents: HashMap<String, Option<HashMap<String, PermissionValue>>>,
}
```

## Public API
```rust
/// Returns true if the last matching rule for "compress" permission is a wildcard with action "deny"
pub fn compress_disabled_by_opencode(configs: &[Option<&HashMap<String, PermissionValue>>]) -> bool;

/// Resolves effective compress permission considering base config + host overrides
pub fn resolve_effective_compress_permission(
    base: PermissionAction,
    host: &HostPermissionSnapshot,
    agent_name: Option<&str>,
) -> PermissionAction;

/// Returns true if the config has an explicit entry for the given tool
pub fn has_explicit_tool_permission(
    config: &Option<HashMap<String, PermissionValue>>,
    tool: &str,
) -> bool;
```

## Internal functions
```rust
/// Glob matching: normalize backslashes, escape regex metachars, convert * and ?
fn wildcard_match(value: &str, pattern: &str) -> bool;

/// Find last matching rule (last-match-wins semantics)
fn find_last_matching_rule<F>(rules: &[PermissionRule], predicate: F) -> Option<&PermissionRule>;

/// Flatten configs into ordered list of rules
fn get_permission_rules(configs: &[Option<&HashMap<String, PermissionValue>>]) -> Vec<PermissionRule>;
```

## Key algorithm: Permission Resolution
1. Multiple `PermissionConfig` layers → flat ordered list of `PermissionRule`
2. Each rule: permission (tool name), pattern (glob), action
3. Search from LAST to FIRST (last match wins)
4. For compress: match via `wildcard_match("compress", rule.permission)`
5. Return true only when matched rule has `pattern == "*"` AND `action == Deny`

## Platform-aware case sensitivity
```rust
fn is_case_sensitive() -> bool {
    !cfg!(target_os = "windows")
}
```

## Tests (include in this bead)
- `test_wildcard_match_star` — `"compress"` matches `"comp*"`
- `test_wildcard_match_glob` — `"read"` matches `"*"`
- `test_wildcard_match_question` — `"read"` matches `"rea?"`
- `test_wildcard_no_match` — `"write"` does not match `"read*"`
- `test_compress_disabled_when_deny_wildcard` — returns true when last rule is deny/*
- `test_compress_not_disabled_when_allow` — returns false when last rule is allow
- `test_last_match_wins` — verify later configs override earlier ones
- `test_agent_specific_override` — per-agent config takes precedence
- `test_no_config_returns_base` — returns base action when no host config
- `test_has_explicit_tool_permission_found` — returns true for existing key
- `test_has_explicit_tool_permission_missing` — returns false for missing key

## Dependencies
- dcp-permissions scaffold (bead mu3)

## Why this matters
This is the core permission resolution engine. Without it, the compress tool cannot determine if it's allowed to run. The last-match-wins semantics and wildcard matching are critical for the OpenCode integration where host-level permissions can override plugin defaults.

title: dcp-prune: deduplicate, purge_errors, stale_file_reads, prune-to-messages applier
description:
**Background & Purpose:**
The three deterministic pruning strategies are the first line of defense against context bloat. They run on every transform (governed by cache stability mode) and make pruning decisions that are applied before the message stream goes to the LLM.

**Strategies (from SPEC.md Section 5):**

1. Deduplicate (Section 5.1):
   - Groups tool calls by signature (tool + normalized params)
   - Keeps only the most recent call per signature
   - Skips: protected tools, protected paths, errored calls
   - Stats: dedup_pruned counter

2. Purge Errors (Section 5.2):
   - For tool calls with status=error, older than N turns
   - Replaces input with placeholder, keeps error message
   - Skips: protected tools
   - Stats: purge_errors_pruned counter

3. Stale File Reads (Section 5.3):
   - Groups by file path (not signature!)
   - Keeps most recent read/write/edit for each path
   - Distinct from dedup — handles different offset/limit params
   - Skips: protected tools, protected paths
   - Stats: stale_file_reads_pruned counter

**Pipeline (from SPEC.md Section 5.4):**


**apply_prune_to_messages:**
- For each tool_call in state.prune.tools: remove or replace input
- For each tool_result matching a pruned call: remove or replace output
- Tool pairing preserved (Section 11.1)

**Dependencies:**
- Crate: crates/dcp-prune/
- Deps: dcp-state, dcp-storage, dcp-protected, dcp-traits
- Dependents: dcp-core

**Tests:**
- T-PR-1 through T-PR-4: dedup
- T-PE-1 through T-PE-4: purge errors
- T-SF-1 through T-SF-4: stale file reads
- T-PI-1 through T-PI-3: pipeline

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All 3 strategies run in correct order
- Cache stability mode respected
- Tool pairing always preserved

title: Phase A1: Implement auth.rs — HTTP Basic Auth
description:
# Phase A1: Implement auth.rs — HTTP Basic Auth

## Background
Port of `lib/auth.ts` from TypeScript upstream. Provides HTTP Basic Authentication for the DCP plugin when communicating with the OpenCode server. The TS version:
- Checks if `OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD` env var is set (secure mode)
- Builds `Basic <base64>` Authorization header from env vars
- Injects the header into SDK client request interceptors

## Target file
`crates/dcp-permissions/src/auth.rs`

## Public API
```rust
/// Returns true if OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD env var is set and non-empty.
pub fn is_secure_mode() -> bool;

/// Builds a "Basic <base64>" Authorization header from env vars.
/// Username defaults to "opencode" via OPENCODE_SERVER_USERNAME.
/// Returns None if no password is set.
pub fn get_authorization_header() -> Option<String>;
```

## Implementation notes
- `is_secure_mode()`: `std::env::var("OPENCODE_SERVER_PASSWORD").is_ok_and(|v| !v.is_empty())`
- `get_authorization_header()`: base64 encode `username:password` using `base64` crate, format as `"Basic {encoded}"`
- Username: `std::env::var("OPENCODE_SERVER_USERNAME").unwrap_or_else(|_| "opencode".to_string())`
- Use `base64::engine::general_purpose::STANDARD.encode()` for encoding
- DO NOT add reqwest dependency — our core is sync. Instead, provide the header string and let callers inject it

## Tests (include in this bead)
- `test_secure_mode_with_password` — set env var, assert true
- `test_secure_mode_without_password` — unset env var, assert false
- `test_authorization_header_format` — assert "Basic <base64>" format
- `test_authorization_header_none_when_no_password` — assert None
- `test_default_username_is_opencode` — assert username defaults correctly
- `test_custom_username` — set OPENCODE_SERVER_USERNAME, verify used

## Dependencies
- `dcp-permissions` crate must be scaffolded first (bead mu3)

## Why this matters
Auth is needed when DCP runs as a plugin inside OpenCode server which may be secured with a password. Without this, the plugin cannot communicate with a secured server.
